author: @RePeteAndMe
text:
10:15 "we need that inertia"
Flywheels are an engineering kluge. They act on a symptom, slowing the rate of slowing instead of eliminating the problem: negative torque.

Permanent horseshoe magnets can be arranged into stators and rotors that add torque during each cylinder's final compression, then claw said torque back from its power stroke's peak.

A four cylinder engine would use a Temporal Torque Transfer Device (TTTD) with two stator and two rotor horseshoe magnets. Propellers and transmissions will have lots less stress to deal with (if I can interest anyone in advancing piston engines).

And the scotch yoke is kinda cool. I prefer what I call an inverted crosshead. The connecting rod's upper end is widened to equal parts of a cylinder-diameter-sized circle, so the connecting rod itself bears on the lower cylinder wall. This removes the piston and upper cylinder wall from the equation both mechanically and thermally while forming a bearing that never stops moving: a rod's rotation is at maximum at TDC and BDC, right when sliding stops.

They're both part of my segregated combustion engine (the highly insulated tungsten combustion chambers are alongside instead of inside the cylinders), which should exceed 2/3 efficiency, especially at 40,000+ feet (the Displacement Adjuster makes that possible by maintaining a 3:1 expansion to compression ratio at any desired working displacement without ever resorting to vacuum (a CVT transmission feeds the DA's crankshaft's output to the engine's crankshaft while directly altering the engine's working displacement).

Let me know if you want to see the engine.
Thumbs up. Your videos are always fun and educating

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: When I made this motor I simply had 2 metal flat bar plates stuck on the shafts sticking out at each end of the piston. There was a 6cm gap between these plates and the ends of the valve rod. The way it worked was that at the end of the stroke the momentum of the piston would push the valve over with this plate and the piston would reverse direction. Basically it was a full  linear motor in operation. It was much simpler and did not need a yoke or crank or any other stuff. The only crank needed was to turn the linear motion into rotary motion if rotary motion was what was needed. Btw making a smaller version out of steel using a lathe and some fancy tools and with a powerful compressor got me an rpm of 6400  before the piston smacked the one end off the cylinder and popped out of the end at high speed :). I guess I did not make it strong enough. A simple one using my idea for the valves and without the rotary valve bit could be printed with a 3D printer as long as the cylinder was made using a smooth plexiglass tube.

replies.author: @kingofnothing2260
replies.text: Of course, but you have to carry pressurized air with you to push something like a truck. It's been tried, but low range and danger of explosion in an accident

replies.author: @dantronics1682
replies.text: @kingofnothing2260 why would there be danger of explosion? trucks already use compress air for its airbrakes and starting and there are trucks and cars that have propane tanks fitted for running on lpg and cng

replies.author: @kingofnothing2260
replies.text: @dantronics1682 a huge reservoir of thousands of psi air would be needed for any real range. Same reason hydrogen cars aren't used. Carbon fiber containment is best under testing as it will split in an accident rather than crumple, but still

replies.author: @kingofnothing2260
replies.text: @dantronics1682 there's a lot of research into air driven cars and lots of reasons they aren't common
